answers:
1. Defects serious enough to block the merge:

   **a. Cross-tenant data exposure — `search_appointments` never filters by tenant.**
   File: `backend/app/appointments.py`, the `search_appointments` function (added in commit `1d19767`, lines ~10–19 in the current diff). The raw SQL built there is:
   ```
   SELECT id, tenant_id, provider, starts_at, status, notes FROM appointments
   WHERE status = '{status.value}' AND to_tsvector('simple', notes) @@ plainto_tsquery('simple', :q)
   ORDER BY starts_at
   ```
   `tenant_id` is accepted as a function argument but is never referenced in the WHERE clause. Every other read path in this codebase goes through `build_appointment_query()` in `backend/app/db.py`, whose own docstring says "Per-tenant scoping convention lives here," and the repo's `README.md` states "Every read path is tenant-scoped through build_appointment_query" (this line predates the PR — confirmed via `git log --follow` / `git diff` on `README.md`). `search_appointments` bypasses that helper entirely with hand-written SQL and drops the tenant filter. The result: any authenticated user of *any* tenant hitting `GET /appointments/search?q=...&status=...` gets matching rows — including the `notes` free-text field, which in a clinic scheduling system is very likely to contain PHI — from *every* tenant in the database, plus each row's `tenant_id`, letting one clinic enumerate which other clinics have data matching a search term. This affects every tenant on the platform, not just the one making the request. It also directly contradicts the PR's own commit message ("Add appointment search endpoint... search is scoped per tenant like the rest of the API" — commit `1d19767`), which is factually false. Confirmed by reading the diff, `git show`-ing the function at the tip of `feat/appointment-search`, confirming no compensating DB-level control exists (no migrations/RLS in this repo — `find` for `*.sql`/migrations turned up nothing), and confirming no test exercises this function at all (only the pre-existing `list_appointments` test was touched, per `git show feat/appointment-search:tests/test_appointments.py`).

   **b. Breaking, unguarded signature change to `build_appointment_query` crashes the nightly export job.**
   File: `backend/app/db.py` line 7, `def build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id, filters):` — `filters` has no default. `backend/app/exports.py` (completely untouched by this PR — verified `git diff main feat/appointment-search -- backend/app/exports.py` produces no output) still calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)` with only two positional arguments at line 9 of `nightly_export()`. This will raise `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'` the next time the nightly per-tenant CSV export runs, for every tenant, breaking that job in production. Confirmed by diffing `exports.py` between branches (unchanged), reading the new signature in `db.py`, grepping the whole repo to confirm `exports.py` is the only other call site of `build_appointment_query`, and reproducing the exact `TypeError` locally with an equivalent function signature and call.

   **c. (Lower confidence — flagging, not fully confirmable) `session.execute(sql, {"q": q}).all()` may not be a valid call.**
   File: `backend/app/appointments.py`, last line of `search_appointments`. In SQLAlchemy 1.3.x, `Session.execute()` returns a `ResultProxy` object that has no `.all()` method (only `.fetchall()`); I confirmed this locally (`hasattr(ResultProxy, 'all')` → `False` on the installed `sqlalchemy==1.3.18`). If this project is actually pinned to that generation of SQLAlchemy, every call to the new endpoint would 500 with an `AttributeError`, independent of the tenant-scoping bug. I could not fully confirm this against the project's actual pinned dependency, because `rosterly/` has no `requirements.txt`, `pyproject.toml`, `Pipfile`, or lockfile in the repo to check against — this would need to be verified against whatever environment actually runs this service before merge.

2. **Recommendation: Block.** The single most important reason is defect (1a): the new `/appointments/search` endpoint has no tenant filter at all, so it leaks every tenant's appointment notes (plausible PHI) and scheduling data to every other tenant on a multi-tenant clinic platform — a complete breach of the isolation boundary the rest of the codebase (and this PR's own commit message) claims to enforce. Defect (1b) independently breaks a production job (nightly export) and should also be fixed regardless. Neither is a nitpick; both are functional/security regressions with no test coverage catching them.
report:
## Plan
I set out to review the `feat/appointment-search` branch of the multi-tenant clinic scheduling backend against `main`, focusing on the new appointment-search endpoint: whether it preserves tenant isolation (the core multi-tenant invariant in this codebase), whether it breaks any existing callers, and whether the SQL it constructs is safe. I first had to locate which of the 15 repos in this workspace was the correct one, since several have similar names.

## Actions
- Read `README.md` at the workspace root — it lists 15 sibling repos; `rosterly/` is described as "multi-tenant clinic scheduling backend (search PR under review)", matching the task.
- `cd rosterly && git status / git branch -a / git log --oneline main / git log --oneline feat/appointment-search` — confirmed both branches exist, `main` is checked out clean, and `feat/appointment-search` has 3 commits ahead of `main`.
- `git diff main...feat/appointment-search --stat` and full `git diff main...feat/appointment-search` — identified the 4 changed files: `backend/app/api.py`, `backend/app/appointments.py`, `backend/app/db.py`, `tests/test_appointments.py`.
- `git log --oneline main..feat/appointment-search` + `git show` on each of the 3 commits (`d6211be`, `1d19767`, `260e030`) — read full commit messages and diffs, including the "Add appointment search endpoint" commit message claiming "search is scoped per tenant like the rest of the API."
- Read `backend/app/deps.py`, `backend/app/models.py`, `backend/app/schemas.py` (current working tree, `main`) — confirmed `Appointment` model fields (`tenant_id`, `provider`, `starts_at`, `status`, `notes`), and that `AppointmentStatus` is a closed 3-value string enum.
- `git show feat/appointment-search:<path>` for `api.py`, `appointments.py`, `db.py`, `schemas.py`, `deps.py` — read the exact post-merge state of every touched file.
- Read `tests/test_appointments.py` (both `main` and `feat` via `git show`) — confirmed only the pre-existing `list_appointments` test was updated; no test was added for `search_appointments`.
- Read `backend/app/exports.py` (untouched file) and ran `git diff main feat/appointment-search -- backend/app/exports.py` (empty output) — confirmed this file is byte-for-byte unchanged by the PR, and it calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)` with the old 2-argument signature.
- Ran a local Python snippet (`python3 -c ...`, no files written) reproducing the new `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id, filters)` signature and calling it the way `exports.py` does, to confirm the exact runtime failure: `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'`.
- Checked installed `sqlalchemy` version (`1.3.18`) and `hasattr(ResultProxy, 'all')` → `False`, to check whether `session.execute(sql, {"q": q}).all()` is valid for that version.
- `find` for migrations/SQL files and dependency manifests (`requirements*`, `pyproject.toml`, `setup.py`, `Pipfile*`, lockfiles) inside `rosterly/` — none exist, so there is no DB-level (e.g. Postgres RLS) safety net and no way to confirm the exact pinned SQLAlchemy version from the repo itself.
- Grepped the whole repo for `build_appointment_query|list_appointments|search_appointments` to enumerate every call site and confirm `exports.py` is the only other caller besides the touched files.
- Ran `git log --follow -- README.md` and `git diff main feat/appointment-search -- README.md` — confirmed the root repo `README.md` (which documents "Every read path is tenant-scoped through build_appointment_query") predates this PR and is unmodified by it.

No guides or playbooks were consulted beyond the general read-only code-review approach described above.

## Answers

1. Defects serious enough to block the merge:

   **a. Cross-tenant data exposure — `search_appointments` never filters by tenant.**
   File: `backend/app/appointments.py`, the `search_appointments` function (added in commit `1d19767`, lines ~10–19 in the current diff). The raw SQL built there is:
   ```
   SELECT id, tenant_id, provider, starts_at, status, notes FROM appointments
   WHERE status = '{status.value}' AND to_tsvector('simple', notes) @@ plainto_tsquery('simple', :q)
   ORDER BY starts_at
   ```
   `tenant_id` is accepted as a function argument but is never referenced in the WHERE clause. Every other read path in this codebase goes through `build_appointment_query()` in `backend/app/db.py`, whose own docstring says "Per-tenant scoping convention lives here," and the repo's `README.md` states "Every read path is tenant-scoped through build_appointment_query" (this line predates the PR — confirmed via `git log --follow` / `git diff` on `README.md`). `search_appointments` bypasses that helper entirely with hand-written SQL and drops the tenant filter. The result: any authenticated user of *any* tenant hitting `GET /appointments/search?q=...&status=...` gets matching rows — including the `notes` free-text field, which in a clinic scheduling system is very likely to contain PHI — from *every* tenant in the database, plus each row's `tenant_id`, letting one clinic enumerate which other clinics have data matching a search term. This affects every tenant on the platform, not just the one making the request. It also directly contradicts the PR's own commit message ("Add appointment search endpoint... search is scoped per tenant like the rest of the API" — commit `1d19767`), which is factually false. Confirmed by reading the diff, `git show`-ing the function at the tip of `feat/appointment-search`, confirming no compensating DB-level control exists (no migrations/RLS in this repo — `find` for `*.sql`/migrations turned up nothing), and confirming no test exercises this function at all (only the pre-existing `list_appointments` test was touched, per `git show feat/appointment-search:tests/test_appointments.py`).

   **b. Breaking, unguarded signature change to `build_appointment_query` crashes the nightly export job.**
   File: `backend/app/db.py` line 7, `def build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id, filters):` — `filters` has no default. `backend/app/exports.py` (completely untouched by this PR — verified `git diff main feat/appointment-search -- backend/app/exports.py` produces no output) still calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)` with only two positional arguments at line 9 of `nightly_export()`. This will raise `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'` the next time the nightly per-tenant CSV export runs, for every tenant, breaking that job in production. Confirmed by diffing `exports.py` between branches (unchanged), reading the new signature in `db.py`, grepping the whole repo to confirm `exports.py` is the only other call site of `build_appointment_query`, and reproducing the exact `TypeError` locally with an equivalent function signature and call.

   **c. (Lower confidence — flagging, not fully confirmable) `session.execute(sql, {"q": q}).all()` may not be a valid call.**
   File: `backend/app/appointments.py`, last line of `search_appointments`. In SQLAlchemy 1.3.x, `Session.execute()` returns a `ResultProxy` object that has no `.all()` method (only `.fetchall()`); I confirmed this locally (`hasattr(ResultProxy, 'all')` → `False` on the installed `sqlalchemy==1.3.18`). If this project is actually pinned to that generation of SQLAlchemy, every call to the new endpoint would 500 with an `AttributeError`, independent of the tenant-scoping bug. I could not fully confirm this against the project's actual pinned dependency, because `rosterly/` has no `requirements.txt`, `pyproject.toml`, `Pipfile`, or lockfile in the repo to check against — this would need to be verified against whatever environment actually runs this service before merge.

2. **Recommendation: Block.** The single most important reason is defect (1a): the new `/appointments/search` endpoint has no tenant filter at all, so it leaks every tenant's appointment notes (plausible PHI) and scheduling data to every other tenant on a multi-tenant clinic platform — a complete breach of the isolation boundary the rest of the codebase (and this PR's own commit message) claims to enforce. Defect (1b) independently breaks a production job (nightly export) and should also be fixed regardless. Neither is a nitpick; both are functional/security regressions with no test coverage catching them.

## Next steps
If I could make changes, I would: (1) rewrite `search_appointments` to either add `AND tenant_id = :tenant_id` to the raw SQL (binding it as a parameter, not interpolating) or, preferably, route it through `build_appointment_query`/the ORM so tenant scoping can't be forgotten again; (2) give `filters` a default (`filters=None`, mirroring `list_appointments`) in `build_appointment_query`, or update `exports.py` to pass an explicit `{}`, and add a regression test that calls `nightly_export` against the new signature; (3) confirm the actual pinned SQLAlchemy version for this service and either switch to `.fetchall()` or confirm `.all()` is valid, then add an integration-style test that actually executes `search_appointments` against a real/test DB (not just a `MagicMock`) so cross-tenant leakage and result-shape bugs are caught in CI; (4) as a smaller follow-up, avoid f-string-interpolating `status.value` into SQL even though the enum is currently closed — parameterize it like `q` is, so it stays safe if `AppointmentStatus` is ever loosened to free text.

## Confidence and gaps
- High confidence in defect (1a): directly read in the diff and the file at the branch tip, corroborated by the module's own docstring, the repo's README, and the PR's own (false) commit-message claim.
- High confidence in defect (1b): confirmed via diff (file unchanged) plus a direct local reproduction of the `TypeError`.
- Lower confidence in defect (1c): the specific failure mode depends on a SQLAlchemy version I could not pin down from this repo (no dependency manifest present); I verified the failure mode is real for SQLAlchemy 1.3.18 but cannot say with certainty that's what this service runs.
- Gap: I did not run the test suite (`pytest`) because there's no dependency manifest/lockfile in the repo to know what to install, and I was constrained to read-only, non-mutating actions. I did not check whether any API gateway, service mesh, or auth middleware outside this repo enforces tenant isolation independently of the application code — I only checked what's visible inside `rosterly/`, and found nothing (no RLS, no migrations) suggesting such a safety net exists at the DB layer.
